Huqqam ʿal ('raised on high/raised to prominence') — the Hophal of qûm (to rise/establish) — anticipates the resurrection-exaltation language the NT applies to Christ. Nĕʾîm (lovely, pleasant, beloved) shares the root with Naomi (pleasant) and Jonathan's lament ('how lovely (nāʿamtā) were you to me,' 2 Sam 1:26).
23.2: Spirit-inspired speech. 'The Spirit of the LORD (rûaḥ YHWH) speaks (dibber) through me; his word (millātô) is on my tongue (ʿal lĕšônî).' The claim is unambiguous prophetic inspiration: the Spirit of YHWH is the grammatical subject of dibber (spoke, perfect tense — the speaking has already occurred and its effect remains). Millāh (word, utterance) is the Aramaic loan-word that appears in Aramaic sections of Daniel and Job; its use here is unusual in Hebrew narrative, possibly reflecting the archaic poetic register of the oracle. 'On my tongue' — the Spirit-word is not merely in David's mind but on his tongue, as in Isa 51:16 (YHWH puts his words in the Servant's mouth).
23.3: The just ruler ideal. 'The God of Israel has spoken (dĭbbēr ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl); the Rock of Israel (ṣûr yiśrāʾēl) has said to me: Whoever rules over men with justice (môšēl bāʾādām ṣaddîq), who rules in the fear of God (mōšēl yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm).' The verse is grammatically difficult — it functions as a conditional clause whose apodosis follows in v4. Ṣaddîq (just, righteous) applied to the ruler is the ideal of Ps 72: 'Give the king your justice (mišpāṭîm), O God, and your righteousness (ṣidqāh) to the royal son.' The 'fear of God' as the governing principle of the just ruler is the root of all covenant ethics (Prov 1:7: 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom').
23.4: Light-of-morning simile. 'He is like the light of morning (kəʾôr bōqer), like sunrise on a cloudless day (šemeš bōqer), like the gleam of rain (minnōgah) on green grass (dešeʾ) after showers.' Three similes describe the effect of the just ruler: morning light (dispelling darkness), sunrise on clear sky (unobstructed brilliance), and rain-gleam on grass (the combination of sun and moisture that makes growth possible). Nōgah (brightness, gleam) is the same word used for the halo around the divine chariot-throne in Ezek 1:4, 27-28. The ideal ruler's effect on the land is like the first light of dawn — he brings life and clarity after the darkness of oppression.
23.5: Everlasting covenant. 'Is it not true that my house stands so before God? For he has made with me an everlasting covenant (bĕrît ʿôlām), ordered (ʿărûḵāh) and secured (šəmûrāh) in every way.' The verse is syntactically difficult: the opening kî lōʾ is either negative ('surely not so is my house with God') or strongly asseverative ('surely thus is my house with God'). The context and the confident declaration that follows suggest the asseverative reading. Bĕrît ʿôlām (everlasting covenant) is the technical term for the unconditional Davidic covenant of 2 Sam 7 (also Isa 55:3; Ps 89:28). 'Ordered and secured' (ʿărûḵāh ûšəmûrāh) — the covenant is organized and guarded, reliable at every point. 'All my salvation and all my desire' — the covenant is the ground of all confident expectation.
23.6: Worthless men like thorns. 'But worthless men (bəliyyaʿal) are like thorns (qôṣ) that are cast away (munādîm), for they cannot be taken up with the hand.' Bĕliyyaʿal (Belial, worthlessness) is the same characterization of Eli's sons (1 Sam 2:12) and Nabal (1 Sam 25:25) — those who operate entirely outside covenant norms. The thorn-imagery for the wicked appears in Ezek 28:24 ('a brier or a thorn' for hostile nations) and Mic 7:4. Thorns that cannot be handled safely are discarded — the worthless have no useful function in the covenant community and are ultimately removed from it.
23.7: Iron and fire for the thorns. 'Whoever touches them must be armed with iron (barzel) and the shaft of a spear (ʿēṣ ḥanît), and they are utterly burned up (śārôp yiśśārĕpû) where they stand.' The handling of thorns requires armored protection — iron and spear-shaft as protection against their snagging. 'Utterly burned' uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis (śārôp yiśśārĕpû, burning they are burned) — the complete and irreversible incineration of the worthless. Judgment on the covenant-breakers is not gradual but total. This verse closes the oracle with a judicial note: the just ruler's reign (vv3-5) means the end of the worthless (vv6-7).
23.8: Josheb-basshebeth and the eight hundred / three hundred problem. 'Josheb-basshebeth the Tahchemonite, chief of the Three. He raised his spear (ḥănîtô) against eight hundred (šĕmōneh mēʾôt) men and killed them in a single battle.' The parallel in 1 Chr 11:11 reads 'three hundred' rather than 'eight hundred' — one of the clearest numerical discrepancies between Samuel and Chronicles. The Chronicler typically reduces numbers (a tendency also seen in census figures); some MSS of LXX have intermediate numbers. The MT of 2 Sam 22 ('eight hundred') is likely the earlier reading. The feat, however numbered, establishes the category of warrior whose single-handed lethality qualifies him for the honor list.
23.9: Eleazar at Pas-dammim. 'Next to him among the three warriors was Eleazar son of Dodo son of Ahohi. He was with David when they taunted (ḥērĕpû) the Philistines gathered for battle at Pas-dammim.' Ḥārap (to taunt, reproach, defy) is the verb used of Goliath's taunting in 1 Sam 17:10, 25, 26, 36 — the defiance that motivates the battle. The connection establishes continuity between David's Goliath victory and his mighty men's later battles: they fight in the same spirit of covenant defiance of YHWH's enemies.
23.10: Hand frozen to the sword. 'He held his ground and struck down Philistines until his hand grew tired and his hand froze to the sword (wattidbaqa yādô ʾel hahereḇ). The LORD brought about a great victory that day.' The graphic detail of the hand seizing to the sword-grip in prolonged combat is one of the most vivid military images in the OT. 'YHWH brought about a great victory' (waYYHWH ʿāśāh tĕšûʿāh gĕdôlāh) — the divine agent is explicitly credited with the outcome, while Eleazar's action is the human instrument. The pattern of human effort completed by divine victory characterizes all the mighty men narratives.
23.11: Shammah at the lentil field. 'Next was Shammah son of Agee the Hararite. The Philistines had gathered at Lehi (Leḥî), where there was a field full of lentils (ʿădāšîm).' Lehi ('jawbone') is the site of Samson's slaughter of the Philistines in Judg 15:9-17 — the same location sanctifies a later Philistine confrontation. Lentils are a common crop in Judah (red lentils = the Esau/Jacob transaction in Gen 25:34; lentils in David's provisions in 2 Sam 17:28). The detail of the crop establishes that this is a battle to defend ordinary agricultural life — the food security of YHWH's people at stake in the military standoff.
23.12: Shammah's stand and YHWH's victory. 'But he planted himself (wayyityaṣṣēb) in the middle of the field, defended it (wayyaṣṣîlehā), and struck down the Philistines. The LORD brought about a great victory.' Yāṣab (hithpael: to position/plant oneself firmly) is the military term for taking a stand — the same root used in 1 Sam 17:16 (Goliath 'stationed himself' every day). The repetition of the YHWH-victory formula (also v10) bookends the three warriors' individual stories and insists that the honor belongs to YHWH: the warriors are instruments of divine deliverance, not autonomous heroes.
23.13: The cave of Adullam and Bethlehem. 'Three of the thirty chief warriors went down at harvest time and came to David at the cave of Adullam (mĕʿārat ʿAdullām), while a Philistine raiding band was encamped in the Valley of Rephaim (ʿēmeq rĕpāʾîm).' The cave of Adullam is David's primary refuge in his fugitive period (1 Sam 22:1-2); its mention here recalls those desperate years and establishes the bond between David and these men as forged in adversity. The Valley of Rephaim (modern Baqa'a, west of Jerusalem) is the battle-site of 2 Sam 5:17-25 — providing a possible geographical connection to the Baal-perazim victory.
23.14: David in the stronghold, Philistines at Bethlehem. 'David was in the stronghold (mĕṣûdāh) at the time, and the Philistine garrison (nĕṣîḇ) was at Bethlehem.' The mĕṣûdāh may be the cave of Adullam or a different refuge; Bethlehem is David's hometown (1 Sam 16:1, 18; 17:58). The Philistine occupation of Bethlehem — the city of David's birth and family — intensifies the emotional resonance of his longing in v15. The garrison (nĕṣîḇ) is a military post, an occupying force maintaining Philistine control over the Judean countryside.
23.15: David's longing for water. 'David was overcome with longing (wayyitʾawweh dawid) and said, “O that someone would bring me water to drink from the well at Bethlehem's gate!”' The verb ʾāwāh (to long, crave, desire) in the hithpael is intense longing — the same root used in Num 11:4 for the mixed multitude's craving and in Deut 12:20 for legitimate desire. The desire for water from the specific well at Bethlehem's gate is simultaneously a military impossibility (behind enemy lines) and an intensely personal, nostalgic yearning — the longing of a man separated from his homeland by war.
23.16: The water poured out to YHWH. 'Then the three warriors broke through (wayyibbĕqĕʿû) the Philistine lines, drew water from the well at Bethlehem's gate, and carried it back to David. But he refused (wayyĕmaʾēn) to drink it. He poured it out (wayassĕḵēhû) to the LORD.' David's refusal and libation is one of the most theologically profound acts in the narrative. The pouring out of water as a libation (nāsaḵ) to YHWH is a sacrificial act (1 Sam 7:6); the libation acknowledges that what was obtained at the risk of life is sacred — too precious for personal use and belonging to YHWH. The blood-equivalent status of the water (explicitly stated in v17) makes the libation a kind of non-blood atonement.
23.17: Blood of the men. 'And said, “Far be it from me, O LORD, to do this! Is this not the blood (dām) of the men who went at the risk of their own lives (bĕnapšôtām)?” So he would not drink it.' David explicitly calls the water 'the blood of the men' — the equation of water obtained at mortal risk with the men's own lifeblood. Bĕnapšôtām (with their lives/souls) identifies the water as the men's nepheš (life-force) itself — if blood is the seat of life (Lev 17:11), then water won at mortal peril is functionally equivalent to blood. David's refusal to consume it and his libation-to-YHWH is an act of theological precision: he will not consume what belongs to YHWH as the Lord of life.
23.18: Abishai and his naming alongside the Three. 'Abishai, the brother of Joab son of Zeruiah, was head of the Thirty (rōʾš haššəlōšāh).' A text-critical issue: MT reads 'the Three' (haššəlōšāh) rather than 'the Thirty' (haššəlōšîm); LXX supports 'the Thirty.' The distinction matters: Abishai is either chief of the inner Three (the elite) or head of the broader Thirty. The following verse specifies he 'did not attain to the Three,' which would be contradictory if he were 'head of the Three.' The MKT follows the Thirty reading as more consistent. 'He raised his spear against three hundred men, killed them, and earned a name (wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô) alongside the Three.'
23.19: Abishai's honor without membership. 'He was the most honored of the Thirty (nikkbāḏ miššəlōšîm) and became their commander, but he did not attain to the Three (wĕʾel haššəlōšāh lōʾ bāʾ).' The formal hierarchy is: the Three (elite inner circle) > the Thirty (broader honor guard) > the general army. Abishai occupies the highest position within the Thirty but cannot reach the Three's level. The honest recording of who attained the highest honors and who did not — without embarrassment about the gradations — reflects the honor-culture of the ancient warrior society.
23.20: Benaiah's exploits. 'Benaiah son of Jehoiada was a valiant warrior (ben ʾîš ḥayil) from Kabzeel, a man of great deeds (rab pĕʿālîm). He struck down two warriors of Moab (ʾărîʾēl môʾāb). He also went down and killed a lion in a pit on a day when snow had fallen.' ʾĂrîʾēl (probably 'hero of God' or 'lion of God') is a rare word used also as a name for Jerusalem (Isa 29:1-2). The lion-in-pit-in-snow exploit is remarkable for its specificity of circumstance: the snow made the terrain treacherous, the pit confined the lion — the odds were maximized against Benaiah, and he prevailed anyway.
23.21: The Egyptian's spear. 'He also struck down a formidable Egyptian (ʾîš miṣrî mar'eh). The Egyptian had a spear (ḥanît) in his hand, but Benaiah went against him with a club (šēḇeṭ), snatched the spear from his hand, and killed him with his own spear.' The reverse-weapon motif — taking the enemy's weapon and killing him with it — recalls David and Goliath's sword (1 Sam 17:51). The irony of the superior weapon turned against its owner is a recurring literary trope expressing divine reversal: the powerful are undone by their own instruments. Benaiah's club vs. the Egyptian's spear is a deliberate underdog scenario.
23.22: Benaiah's name alongside the Three. 'These were the things Benaiah son of Jehoiada did, and he won a name alongside the three warriors.' The name-winning (šēm) formula is the ancient honor culture's acknowledgment: a man's deeds earn him a permanent reputation (šēm). The same šēm-formula appears in Gen 6:4 for the Nephilim ('men of renown, the men of name') — indicating that the category of famous warrior is archaic. That Benaiah wins a name 'alongside the Three' without being one of them is another precise gradation of honor.
23.23: Benaiah over the bodyguard. 'He was more honored than the Thirty, but he did not attain to the Three. David placed him in charge of his bodyguard (mišmaʿ).' Benaiah's subsequent career confirms his elite status: he executes Joab (1 Kgs 2:34) and Shimei (1 Kgs 2:46) for Solomon and is appointed commander of the army in Joab's place (1 Kgs 2:35) — the highest military office in Israel. His placement over the mišmaʿ (the royal guard, literally 'listening/compliance force') is the first step in a career that will define Solomon's reign.
23.24: Opening of the Thirty list. 'Asahel the brother of Joab was one of the Thirty; Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem.' The list includes Asahel, killed by Abner in 2 Sam 2:23 — his presence in the Thirty means the list preserves names of men who served in David's early career, including those who died in the civil war period. Elhanan of Bethlehem is noteworthy: the 'Elhanan who killed Goliath' tradition (2 Sam 21:19; 1 Chr 20:5 corrects to 'Lahmi, brother of Goliath') may reflect the same individual or a different Elhanan from the same city.
23.25: Shammah and Elika of Harod. 'Shammah of Harod, Elika of Harod.' Harod is the spring where Gideon's army was tested (Judg 7:1 — 'the spring of Harod'), which flows at the northern foot of Mount Gilboa. The region of Harod/Gilboa is militarily significant as the site of both Gideon's victory and Saul's defeat (1 Sam 31). Men from this region in David's honor guard represent the incorporation of Saul's former territory into the Davidic military structure.
23.26: Helez and Ira. 'Helez the Paltite, Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa.' Tekoa is Amos's hometown (Amos 1:1) and the city from which Joab recruits the wise woman in 2 Sam 14:2. Ira the Tekoite thus comes from a region associated with wisdom and prophecy. 'Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa' appears in the monthly rotation of 1 Chr 27:9 as commander of the ninth monthly division — his Thirty membership is confirmed in the administrative lists.
23.27: Abiezer and Mebunnai. 'Abiezer of Anathoth, Mebunnai the Hushathite.' Anathoth is the priestly city in Benjamin, later the birthplace of Jeremiah (Jer 1:1). An Abiezer of Anathoth in the Thirty suggests the military integration of priestly-tribal territories. 'Mebunnai the Hushathite' is likely the same as Sibbechai the Hushathite (2 Sam 21:18; 1 Chr 11:29: Sibbechai), who slew Saph/Sippai the Philistine — a scribal variant between Mĕḇunnay and Sibḇĕḵay.
23.28: Zalmon and Maharai. 'Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai of Netophah.' Netophah (modern Khirbet Bedd Faluh, south of Bethlehem) appears in the post-exilic return lists (Ezra 2:22; Neh 7:26) as a significant Judean village. Two Netophathites appear in the Thirty (Maharai, v28, and Heleb, v29), suggesting a concentration of military talent in that village. The 'Ahohite' designation (Zalmon, Eleazar in v9) connects to the clan of Ahoh in Benjamin (1 Chr 8:4).
23.29: Heleb and Ittai. 'Heleb son of Baanah of Netophah, Ittai son of Ribai of Gibeah of Benjamin.' Gibeah of Benjamin is Saul's city (1 Sam 10:26; 11:4) — Ittai son of Ribai from Gibeah in David's Thirty represents the absorption of Saul's tribal homeland into Davidic military service. This is politically significant: Benjamin, the tribe most resistant to David (the Sheba revolt of 2 Sam 20 is led by a Benjaminite), nevertheless contributes men to David's elite guard.
23.30: Benaiah and Hiddai. 'Benaiah of Pirathon, Hiddai of the wadis of Gaash.' Pirathon is in the hill country of Ephraim (Judg 12:15 — home of the judge Abdon). Benaiah of Pirathon (not the same as Benaiah son of Jehoiada, v20) appears in the monthly rotation of 1 Chr 27:14. The 'wadis of Gaash' appear in Josh 24:30 and Judg 2:9 as the burial site of Joshua — a geographical marker connecting David's warriors to the conquest generation's inheritance.
23.31: Abi-albon and Azmaveth. 'Abi-albon the Arbathite, Azmaveth of Bahurim.' Bahurim is the village where Shimei cursed David during the Absalom revolt (2 Sam 16:5) — having an elite warrior from Bahurim in the Thirty suggests that this Benjaminite village had mixed loyalties. Azmaveth of Bahurim is also listed in 1 Chr 11:33 as Azmaveth the Baharumite; his name appears in 2 Sam 23:31 without the variant. Azmaveth is also the name of David's chief treasurer (1 Chr 27:25 — the same individual or a namesake).
23.32: Eliahba and Jonathan. 'Eliahba the Shaalbonite, the sons of Jashen, Jonathan.' 'The sons of Jashen' is a textual problem — 1 Chr 11:34 reads 'the sons of Hashem the Gizonite' and the name Jonathan appears as the son of Shage the Hararite. The MT here gives 'the sons of Jashen (bənê yāšēn), Jonathan' — multiple sons of Jashen plus a Jonathan. The textual confusion likely reflects scribal error (haplography or metathesis) in one of the two traditions.
23.33: Shammah the Hararite and Ahiam. 'Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite.' The 'Hararite' gentilic appears three times in the Thirty (Shammah son of Agee in v11, and two in v33) — a clan or geographical designation (possibly 'of the hill/mountain,' from hār). Shammah of Harod (v25) and Shammah the Hararite (v33) are two distinct individuals sharing a common name.
23.34: Eliphelet and Eliam. 'Eliphelet son of Ahasbai of Maacah, Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite.' Eliam son of Ahithophel is the only person in the list identifiable by family connection to a named figure: Ahithophel is David's chief counselor who defected to Absalom (2 Sam 15:12; 16:20-17:23). If Eliam is also the father of Bathsheba (2 Sam 11:3), then Eliam is the son of Ahithophel and father of Bathsheba — which would make Ahithophel Bathsheba's grandfather, and his betrayal of David potentially motivated by revenge for David's treatment of his granddaughter.
23.35: Hezro and Paarai. 'Hezro of Carmel, Paarai the Arbite.' Carmel (near Maon in Judah) is Nabal's estate (1 Sam 25:2) and the site of Abigail's encounter with David — a geographical connection between the Thirty and the David-Abigail narrative. 1 Chr 11:37 reads 'Naarai son of Ezbai' where 2 Sam 23:35 has 'Paarai the Arbite' — another textual variant between the two lists.
23.36: Igal and Bani. 'Igal son of Nathan of Zobah, Bani the Gadite.' 'Son of Nathan of Zobah' — this Nathan is presumably a Zobahite, not the prophet Nathan. Zobah (Aram-Zobah) is the Aramean kingdom David defeated (2 Sam 8:3-8; 10:6-19) — having a Zobahite in the Thirty indicates that Davidic military service incorporated men from conquered territories. Bani the Gadite represents the Transjordanian tribes in the honor roll.
23.37: Zelek and Naharai. 'Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai of Beeroth — the armor-bearer (nōśēʾ kēlîm) of Joab son of Zeruiah.' Zelek the Ammonite is one of two explicitly foreign members of the Thirty (the other being Uriah the Hittite, v39), confirming that David's elite guard included non-Israelites on the basis of personal loyalty and military valor. Naharai of Beeroth as Joab's armor-bearer establishes a personal connection between the Thirty and Joab's command structure — the armor-bearer of the commander is himself an elite warrior.
23.38: Ira and Gareb the Ithrites. 'Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite.' The Ithrites are a clan of Kiriath-jearim (1 Chr 2:53). Their presence in the Thirty reflects David's draw on talent from across Judah and Benjamin — including those from the area where the ark rested at Abinadab's house (1 Sam 7:1-2; 2 Sam 6:3-4), suggesting possible cultic connection to the ark-transport tradition.
23.39: Uriah the Hittite — the final name. 'Uriah the Hittite — thirty-seven in all.' Uriah the Hittite is named last in the Thirty, after all other warriors. He is the most faithful of David's mighty men — his insistence on maintaining the military abstinence code while David committed adultery with his wife (2 Sam 11:6-13) is a sustained act of integrity that made his king's guilt all the more glaring. The summary count of thirty-seven matches the list as given. Uriah's position as the final name in the honor roll of the greatest warriors in David's service is the narrator's quiet indictment: David murdered the man whose name the list ends with. Matthew 1:6 will permanently preserve this indictment in the genealogy of Jesus.

1.1: The opening phrase wayhî ʾaḥᵃrê môt šāʾûl — 'Now it came to pass after the death of Saul' — uses the narrative waw-consecutive perfect (wayhî) to anchor the entire book of 2 Samuel within the 1 Samuel narrative. The clause is the same formula used in Josh 1:1 after Moses's death, signaling a moment of succession and new divine initiative. The narrator's temporal anchoring ('two days in Ziklag') creates exact chronological continuity with 1 Sam 30:1.
1.2: The mourning signs — bᵉgādāyw qᵉrûʿîm wᵉʾădāmāh ʿal-rōʾšô, clothes torn and earth (ʾădāmāh) on his head — are the standard ANE lamentation gestures (see also Job 2:12; Josh 7:6). The word ʾădāmāh here is precisely soil/earth, not the common ʿāpār (dust); the man has placed actual ground-dirt on his head as a symbol of desolation and mortality.
1.3: David's interrogation formula — mēʾayin tāḇôʾ, 'where have you come from?' — is a standard narrative inquiry used throughout the Samuel narrative (cf. 1 Sam 25:11; 30:13). The Amalekite's self-identification as a survivor from 'the camp of Israel' (maḥănēh yiśrāʾēl) is ambiguous: he positions himself as affiliated with Israel, setting up the theological problem of his subsequent admission.
1.4: The report uses the verb nûs (fled) for the troops and nāpal (fell/died) for the casualties — the same vocabulary as 1 Sam 31:1. The Amalekite's account essentially summarizes 1 Sam 31:1-6, though with crucial divergences in the killing of Saul. The word nāpal carries both the sense of 'fell in battle' and the theological resonance of a fall from divine favor.
1.5: David's follow-up question — ʾêḵ yādaʿtā, 'how do you know' — presses for verification before acting. This caution mirrors the judicial principle in Deut 17:6 (requiring two or three witnesses). The narrative is signaling that David is establishing the evidential basis before drawing conclusions about the LORD's anointed.
1.6: The Amalekite's account diverges from the 1 Sam 31 narrator's version: there, Saul falls on his own sword (1 Sam 31:4); here the Amalekite claims to have killed him at Saul's request. The phrase nišʿan ʿal-ḥᵃnîtô — 'leaning on his spear' — appears in the narrator's description in 1 Sam 31:3-4, lending some authenticity to the report. Whether the man is lying for reward or telling the truth is a classic interpretive crux, but the narrative evaluates his claim by his own words (v.16).
1.7: The particle hinnenî — 'here I am' — is the standard response to a superior's call, used throughout the OT for moments of readiness before a superior or God (Gen 22:1; 1 Sam 3:4-6). The Amalekite's use of it to Saul signals his readiness to serve the king — and thus his active participation in the subsequent events, whatever actually happened.
1.8: Saul's question mî ʾāttāh — 'who are you?' — and the Amalekite's answer ʿAmlēqî ʾānōḵî — 'I am an Amalekite' — is the narrative's sharpest irony. Saul's downfall began with his failure to fully destroy the Amalekites (1 Sam 15); now an Amalekite stands at his death. The ethnic marker here carries the full theological weight of 1 Sam 15:3 and 28:18.
1.9: The Amalekite quotes Saul's death-request: ʿomdāh-nāʾ ʿālây ûmōtᵉtênî — 'stand over me and kill me.' The reason given — kî ʾᵃḥāzanî haššᵉḇāṣ — contains the noun šᵉḇāṣ, a near-hapax (appearing only here and possibly Ps 73:6) of disputed meaning: 'anguish,' 'dizziness,' 'convulsion,' or 'cramp.' The MKT renders 'anguish' — this captures the semantic range better than 'agony' (which implies physical pain primarily) or 'dizziness' (too mild). The phrase kî-ḵol-ʿôd napšî bî, 'for my life still clings to me,' uses nepeš in its primary sense of animating life-force, not soul: Saul's life has not yet departed despite his severe wounds.
1.10: The Amalekite's account of taking the royal insignia: nezer (crown/diadem) and ʾeṣʿādāh (armband). The nezer is the same term used for the high priestly diadem (Exod 29:6; Lev 8:9) and for the Nazirite's consecration crown (Num 6:7) — it marks a person set apart and consecrated to YHWH. Bringing it to David is implicitly a transfer of royal legitimacy, but also a dangerous claim to have handled the person of the LORD's anointed. The narrator does not evaluate the claim's truth; the theological verdict comes in David's response (v.14).
1.11: David's immediate response — wayyaḥᵃzēq dāwiḏ biḇgādāyw wayyiqrāʿēm, 'David took hold of his clothes and tore them' — is genuine mourning, not performance. The verb ḥāzaq (to grasp firmly) before the tearing intensifies the gesture. The mourning extends to 'all the men with him' (kol-hāʾᵃnāšîm ʾᵃšer ʾittô) — solidarity in grief is a narrative marker of authentic communal response.
1.12: The mourning sequence — wayyispeḏû wayyiḇkû wayyāṣûmû — uses three waw-consecutive imperfects: they lamented, wept, and fasted. The verb sāpaḏ is the technical term for formal mourning-rites, including breast-beating and rhythmic lamentation; it is the root from which Zechariah's 'mourning for him whom they have pierced' (wᵉsāpeḏû ʿālāyw, Zech 12:10) is drawn. The fasting 'until evening' (ʿad-hāʿereḇ) is the standard diurnal marker for mourning fasts (Judg 20:26; 1 Sam 31:13).
1.13: David's follow-up identification of the Amalekite — ben-ʾîš gēr ʿᵃmālēqî, 'son of a resident alien, an Amalekite' — uses the legal category gēr (resident foreigner). The gēr lived under Israelite law and enjoyed its protections, but was not a full Israelite. His identity as both gēr (thus subject to Israelite norms) and Amalekite (thus carrying theological freight) is the double basis for David's verdict in v.16.
1.14: David's charge is framed around the theological category of the anointed: lō yārēʾtā liqlōaḥ yādᵉḵā lᵉšaḥēt ʾet-mᵉšîaḥ YHWH — 'you were not afraid to lift your hand to destroy the anointed of the LORD.' The term mᵉšîaḥ (anointed one) denotes someone whose person has been consecrated by the rite of anointing with oil — a rite that transfers them from the common to the sacred sphere. Throughout the David narrative, this category functions as an absolute protection: 1 Sam 24:6, 10; 26:9, 11, 23. David consistently refuses to raise his hand against Saul on this ground. The sacral inviolability of the anointed is not political calculation but theological principle: YHWH's act of anointing creates an obligation that supersedes all other considerations.
1.15: The execution order — lēḵ pᵉgaʿ-bô, 'go, strike him down' — uses pāgaʿ (to fall upon, strike), the same verb used in 1 Sam 22:17-18 for Doeg's killing of the priests. The immediate compliance and the simple report of death (wayyakkēhû wayyāmōt) is narrative shorthand: judicial death without extended account.
1.16: The verdict formula: dāmᵉḵā ʿal-rōʾšᵉḵā — 'your blood is on your own head.' This idiom (dām + ʿal + rōʾš) shifts bloodguilt formally from the executioner to the executed: it is the language of Levitical law regarding self-incrimination and bloodguilt transfer. The Amalekite has condemned himself by his own mouth (kî pîḵā ʿānāh bᵉḵā, 'your own mouth testified against you') — his admission of killing the LORD's anointed is the basis of the verdict, not David's independent knowledge of what actually happened.
1.17: The narrative transition to the lament uses wayyəqōnēn — the piel of qûn, to chant a dirge. The qînāh (dirge-elegy) is a specific literary form distinguished by its 3+2 metrical pattern (longer half-line + shorter half-line), named after this word. The form itself enacts mourning through rhythmic imbalance — the shortened second colon mimics incompleteness, the truncation of life.
1.18: David commands the lament to be taught to 'the sons of Judah' (bᵉnê yᵉhûḏāh) and notes it is 'written in the Book of Jashar' (sēper hayyāšār). The verb lᵉlammed (to teach, piel infinitive construct) indicates this is deliberate cultural formation: official mourning poetry to be memorized and performed. The sēper hayyāšār is also cited in Josh 10:13; it appears to have been an anthology of heroic poetry about Israel's leaders, now lost. The title of the poem — haqqeŝet, 'the bow' — is named for Jonathan's weapon (v.22), not merely for the literary instruction 'to teach the bow.'
1.19: The opening line of the dirge: haṣṣᵉḇî yiśrāʾēl ʿal-bāmôtêḵā ḥālāl — 'your beauty/glory, O Israel, lies slain on your high places.' The noun ṣᵉḇî means both 'gazelle' (the animal) and 'glory/beauty/honor' — the same ambiguity as in v.19 and 2 Sam 23:1 (nᵉʾûm haggeber huqqam ʿāl). The 'high places' (bāmôt) is the topographic term for elevated worship/battle sites; Gilboa is specified later (v.21). The refrain ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm — 'how the mighty have fallen' — uses the exclamatory ʾêḵ (how!) of lament poetry, also in Lam 1:1; 2:1; 4:1.
1.20: The honor-shame geography of the prohibition: ʾal-taggîḏû ḇᵉḡat ʾal-tᵉḇaśśᵉrû bᵉḥûṣôt ʾašqᵉlôn — 'tell it not in Gath, proclaim it not in the streets of Ashkelon.' Gath and Ashkelon are Philistine cities; Gath most proximate to Israelite territory. The double prohibition uses the jussive (ʾal + imperfect) — a softer prohibition implying 'would that it were not' — and the paired verbs nāgad (to tell) and bāśar (to bring news/tidings) are both technically positive report verbs here used negatively. The daughters of the Philistines rejoicing (tiśmaḥnāh) inverts the standard Israelite victory song pattern (1 Sam 18:6-7).
1.21: The curse on Gilboa: hārê baggillᵉbōaʿ ʾal-ṭal wᵉʾal-māṭār ʿᵃlêḵem — 'Mountains of Gilboa, let neither dew nor rain fall on you.' This imprecatory formula uses the jussive and echoes the cosmic judgment language of Deut 11:17 and 1 Kgs 8:35, where withheld rain signals divine disfavor. The phrase śᵉḏê tᵉrûmōt (NIV 'fields of grain offerings'; MKT 'fields yield first-fruit offerings') is difficult: tᵉrûmāh normally denotes a contribution/heave-offering, not a field product. The line may mean: let there be no agricultural produce to offer — curse of barrenness on the killing ground. The clause māgēn šāʾûl bᵉlî māšîaḥ baššāmen — 'the shield of Saul, no longer anointed with oil' — treats the battle-shield as a consecrated object that receives ritual oiling (1 Sam 21:9 notes Goliath's spear; ANE shields were oiled for maintenance and possibly ritual protection). Saul's shield now lies unoiled — abandoned and desacralized.
1.22: The paired weapons line: middam ḥᵃlālîm meḥēleḇ gibbôrîm qeŝet yᵉhônātān lōʾ nāšôg ʾāḥôr — 'from the blood of the slain, from the fat of the warriors, the bow of Jonathan turned not back.' The verse celebrates military effectiveness through the image of weapons sated with enemy blood and fat. The meḥēleḇ ('from the fat') employs the same term as animal sacrifice (ḥēleḇ, the fat portions offered to YHWH) — a subtle elevation of battle prowess to the language of sacred offering. The bow of Jonathan 'did not turn back' (lōʾ nāšôg) — nāšag means to recede or retreat, usually used of armies; the personification of the weapon as a faithful warrior is a characteristic feature of martial poetry.
1.23: The elegiac portrait of Saul and Jonathan together: hammᵉʾāhāḇîm wᵉhanneʿîmīm bᵉḥayyêhem ûḇᵉmôtām lōʾ niprāḏû — 'beloved and lovely in life, and in death they were not separated.' The participle mᵉʾāhāḇîm (beloved) is the passive pual, implying their belovedness was recognized by others; neʿîmîm (lovely/pleasant) is the adjective from the root used in Ps 16:6 ('pleasant lines') and Song 1:16. The comparison to eagles (minnešārîm qallû, 'swifter than eagles') and lions (mēʾᵃrāyôt gāḇērû, 'stronger than lions') deploys Israel's standard martial excellence idiom.
1.24: The call to the daughters of Israel (bᵉnôt yiśrāʾēl) to weep for Saul — hammalbiššᵉḵem šānî ʿim-ʿáḏānîm, 'who clothed you in scarlet with ornaments' — shifts from martial to patronage imagery. Saul as provider of luxury clothing for the women of Israel is a direct reversal of the honor-shame logic of v.20 (the daughters of the Philistines). The king's largesse (ʿáḏānîm, from the root meaning pleasure/luxury) is framed as a reason for public mourning — the patron-benefactor logic that structures ANE kingship.
1.25: The repetition of ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm — the refrain from v.19 — with the addition of bᵉtôḵ hammilḥāmāh, 'in the thick of battle.' The Hebrew prepositional phrase bᵉtôḵ (in the midst of) intensifies the pathos: Jonathan did not die on the margins or in retreat but at the center of the fight.
1.26: The most intimate verse of the lament: ṣar-lî ʿālêḵā ʾāḥî yᵉhônātān nāʿamtā-llî mᵉʾōḏ niplᵉʾātāh ʾahᵃḇātᵉḵā lî middᵉʾahᵃḇat nāšîm — 'I grieve for you, my brother Jonathan; you were most dear to me; your love for me was more extraordinary than the love of women.' The word niplᵉʾātāh is the nifal perfect of pālaʾ — 'to be extraordinary, surpassing, wonderful' — the same root used in Ps 139:6 ('too wonderful for me') and Exod 34:10 ('wonderful things'). The phrase does not make a comparative judgment about love itself but about its quality: covenant-loyalty love (ʾahᵃḇāh in the covenantal sense established in 1 Sam 18:1-4) that is extraordinary in its constancy — Jonathan's giving up his royal rights (1 Sam 18:4) and repeated protection of David at personal risk constitutes this niplāʾ quality.
1.27: The closing refrain: ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm wayyōʾḇᵉḏû kᵉlê milḥāmāh — 'how the mighty have fallen, and the weapons of war perished!' The verb ʾāḇad (perished) echoes the noun form in many Deuteronomic judgment texts (wᵉʾāḇadtā, 'you will perish'); here the weapons of war are treated as agents whose perishing (kᵉlê milḥāmāh) is the natural end of their wielders' fall. The inclusio formed by vv.19 and 27 (both using ʾêḵ nāpᵉlû gibbôrîm) marks the formal boundaries of the Qinah poem.

2.1: The narrative resumes with wayhî ʾaḥᵃrê-ḵēn — 'after this' — and immediately establishes David's mode of decision-making: wayyišʾal dāwiḏ baYHWH, 'David inquired of the LORD.' The idiom šāʾal baYHWH (or šāʾal bᵉ-) is the technical formula for oracle-consultation through the priestly urim and thummim (cf. 1 Sam 23:2, 4; 30:7-8), not general prayer. The inquiry yields a specific directional answer: ʿᵃlēh, 'go up' — a single-word oracle typical of the urim response (contrast with longer prophetic oracle). David then asks ʾanāh ʾeʿᵃleh, 'where shall I go up?' — a follow-up permitted by the oracle system — and receives 'Hebron' by name. The pattern: action only after divine authorization.
2.2: David's two wives are named: ʾAḥînōʿam hayyizrᵉʿēlît (Ahinoam the Jezreelite) and ʾAḇîgayil ʾēšet nāḇāl haKKarmᵉlî (Abigail, wife of Nabal the Carmelite). The precision of identification by geographic origin and previous marriage is a standard narrative marker; it establishes household-level claims that will matter for succession politics in ch 3.
2.3: The clause ʾîš û-ḇêtô — 'each man with his household' — describes a migration of extended family units, not just fighting men. The verb wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉʿārê ḥeḇrôn ('they settled in the towns around Hebron') uses the standard settlement verb yāšaḇ — to sit/dwell — marking this as permanent relocation, not a temporary encampment.
2.4: The anointing at Hebron: wayyimšᵉḥû šām ʾet-dāwiḏ lᵉmelek ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh — 'they anointed David king over the house of Judah.' This is the second of David's three anointings in the narrative: the first was private at Bethlehem (1 Sam 16:13), this is tribal at Hebron (over Judah only), and the third will be national (2 Sam 5:3). The phrase ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh specifies the scope: tribe of Judah, not all Israel — the divided kingdom is already present. The notice about Jabesh-gilead's burial of Saul inserts a note of loyalty that David will explicitly honor (vv.5-7).
2.5: David's message to Jabesh-gilead: ʾašer ʿᵃśîtem ḥesed im-ʾᵃḏōnêḵem ʿim-šāʾûl — 'you showed steadfast love to your lord Saul.' The noun ḥesed here designates loyal covenant action — the treatment of Saul's body as covenant-obligation to the dead king. The verb qāḇar (to bury) with reference to proper burial is a fundamental covenant-duty in the OT (cf. Deut 34:6; Josh 24:30); failure to bury is a curse (Jer 22:19). David invokes their act of ḥesed as the basis for his blessing.
2.6: David's blessing uses both divine and personal pronouns: wᵉgam-ʾānōḵî ʾeʿᵃśeh ʾitᵉḵem haṭṭôḇāh hazzōʾt — 'I too will repay your kindness.' The parallel — 'may YHWH show you ḥesed and faithfulness (ʾemet)... and I also will repay' — models the patron-client dynamic whereby YHWH and the king function as parallel benefactors. The word pair ḥesed wᵉʾemet (steadfast love and faithfulness) is a fixed formula in the covenant vocabulary (Exod 34:6; Ps 25:10; 89:14), applied here at the political level.
2.7: David's exhortation: wᵉʿattāh tiqzᵃqnāh yᵉdêḵem wihyû liḇnê-ḥāyil — 'now let your hands be strong and be courageous.' The idiom ḥāzaq yādayim (strengthen the hands) is the standard encouragement formula in commissioning speeches (Josh 1:6-9; 10:25; Hag 2:4). The death of Saul is announced not as defeat but as the occasion for renewed initiative: 'Saul your lord is dead, and also the house of Judah has anointed me king over them.' The political context is delicate — David acknowledges Jabesh's loyalty to Saul while announcing his own anointment.
2.8: The counter-narrative: wᵉʾaḇnēr ben-nēr śar-haṣṣāḇāʾ ʾᵃšer lᵉšāʾûl lāqaḥ ʾet-ʾîšbōšet ben-šāʾûl — 'Abner son of Ner, the commander of Saul's army, took Ishbosheth son of Saul.' The verb lāqaḥ (took, seized) implies Abner's initiative and agency — Ishbosheth is the object, not the actor. The place-name Maḥᵃnāyim ('two camps' or 'the camp of God') is east of the Jordan in Gilead — Abner has taken the heir to safety behind the river, establishing a rival power base.
2.9: Abner installs Ishbosheth as king over a geographic list: Gilead, the Ashurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, 'and all Israel.' The phrase wᵉʿal-kol-yiśrāʾēl (and over all Israel) is added at the end, implying that the enumerated regions constitute 'all Israel' — but notably Judah is absent. The contrast with v.4's ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh (house of Judah) establishes the structural tension of the divided kingdom that ch 3 will eventually resolve.
2.10: The chronological notices serve a structural function: Ishbosheth was forty years old when he became king and reigned two years. David at Hebron reigned seven years and six months (v.11). The two timelines do not perfectly synchronize — Ishbosheth's two-year reign overlaps only partially with David's seven-and-a-half — which implies that Abner's power-consolidation preceded Ishbosheth's formal installation by several years. The precise numbers serve as anchors within the overlapping chronologies of a divided succession.
2.11: The notice wayhî mispar hayyāmîm ʾᵃšer hāyāh dāwiḏ melek bᵉḥeḇrôn ʿal-bêt yᵉhûḏāh šeḇaʿ šānîm wᵉšiššāh ḥᵃḏāšîm — 'the number of days that David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah was seven years and six months' — uses mispar hayyāmîm (the count of days) rather than simply šānim (years), emphasizing exactitude. This precision differentiates the Hebron regency from the later united kingship (2 Sam 5:5).
2.12: The civil war opens at the pool of Gibeon (berēḵat giḇʿôn). Gibeon is a significant location in Israelite memory — it is where the sun stood still for Joshua (Josh 10:12-13) and later the site of the tabernacle (1 Chr 21:29). The arrangement — Abner's men from Mahanaim facing Joab's men from David's forces — is a confrontation of two armies of the same people, a civil war staged on sacred ground.
2.13: The phrase wayyipgᵉšû yaḥdāw — 'they met together' — uses pāgaš, the same verb used for hostile encounters (cf. Gen 32:17; 33:8; Exod 4:24). The positioning at opposite sides of the pool (ʾēlleh ʿal-haberēḵāh mizzeh wᵉʾēlleh ʿal-haberēḵāh mizzeh) is the standard pre-battle formation. The narrator is building to the 'contest' proposal of v.14.
2.14: Abner's proposal: yāqûmû-nāʾ hanneʿārîm wîśaḥᵃqû lᵉpānênû — 'let the young men rise and play (śāḥaq) before us.' The verb śāḥaq means play, jest, or perform — it is used for children playing (Zech 8:5), for athletic display (Gen 26:8), but also for the Philistines' entertainment at Samson's expense (Judg 16:25). The noun mišḥāq (derived from this root) appears in v.14's summary. The word choice is darkly ironic: what begins as a 'contest' (mišḥāq — literally 'play') becomes a blood-bath in which all 24 contestants die simultaneously (v.16). The same ironic register is present when Samson is brought out to śāḥaq (Judg 16:25).
2.15: The matched champions: twelve for Benjamin / Ishbosheth, twelve for David. The number twelve likely reflects tribal arithmetic — each side producing one per tribe — though only Benjamin is named for Ishbosheth's side. This is not a random tournament but a ritual representative combat: the twelve are standing in for the respective kingdoms.
2.16: The mutual killing: wayyaḥᵃzēq ʾîš bᵉrōʾš rēʿēhû wᵉḥarbô bᵉṣad rēʿēhû wayyippᵉlû yaḥdāw — 'each man grasped his opponent's head and drove his sword into his opponent's side, and they fell together.' The verb ḥāzaq (to grasp firmly) followed by wayyippᵉlû yaḥdāw (they fell together) — simultaneous mutual destruction. The site is named Ḥelqat haSṣûrîm — 'Field of Flints' or 'Field of Sword-Edges' — a place-name whose meaning (ṣûr = rock/flint; ḥālaṣ = to draw a sword or strip) memorializes the event.
2.17: The battle outcome uses the niphal passive for defeat: wayyinnāgep ʾaḇnēr wᵉʾanšê yiśrāʾēl lipnê ʿaḇdê dāwiḏ — 'Abner and the men of Israel were defeated before David's servants.' The verb nāgap (to strike, smite, defeat) in the niphal — 'were smitten' — is the standard passive of military defeat, often with YHWH as the implied agent (cf. Num 14:42; Judg 20:36).
2.18: The introduction of the three sons of Zeruiah: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. The comparative for Asahel — wᵉʾᵃśāhēl qal bᵉraglāyw kᵉʾaḥad hāṣᵉḇāʾyîm ʾᵃšer baśśāḏeh, 'swift of foot like one of the gazelles in the field' — uses the same animal comparison as the lament in ch.1. The ṣᵉḇî (gazelle) is the OT's standard image of agility and speed (Song 2:9, 17; 1 Chr 12:8). The detail foreshadows his death: his very swiftness is what drives him relentlessly into Abner's killing range.
2.19: Asahel's pursuit is characterized by the phrase lōʾ-nāṭāh lāleḵet ʿal-hayyāmîn wᵉʿal-haśśᵉmōʾl — 'not turning right or left.' This formula (from Deut 5:32; 17:11; Josh 1:7) is typically used for adherence to the Torah, not for pursuit — the ironic use here implies a single-mindedness that becomes fatal. Asahel applies the devotion of a Torah-keeper to a pursuit that Abner explicitly warns will destroy him.
2.20: Abner's identification of his pursuer — hᵃʾattāh zeh ʾᵃśāhēl, 'is that you, Asahel?' — and Asahel's monosyllabic answer ʾānōḵî, 'it is I/I am he,' echoes the pattern of fatal identification scenes in the narrative (cf. 2 Sam 1:8; 1 Kgs 18:7). The exchange serves as the narrative's last moment of possibility for Asahel to turn away.
2.21: Abner's warning: nᵉṭēh lᵉḵā ʿal-yāmînᵉḵā ʾô ʿal-śᵉmōʾleḵā — 'turn aside right or left.' This directly inverts the phrase applied to Asahel in v.19. The offer — 'seize one of the soldiers and take his equipment' (ʾᵃḥōz lᵉḵā ʾeḥāḏ mēhannᵉʿārîm wᵉqaḥ lᵉḵā ʾet-ḥᵃlîṣātô) — is a face-saving alternative: take plunder instead of pursuing a fight you cannot win. The word ḥᵃlîṣāh (armor/equipment) derives from ḥālaṣ (to strip, to equip for war).
2.22: Abner's second warning makes explicit the blood-feud consequence: ʾêḵ ʾeśśāʾ pānay ʾel-yôʾāḇ ʾāḥîḵā — 'how could I lift my face to Joab your brother?' The phrase nāśāʾ pānîm (to lift the face) means to face someone without shame; it implies that killing Asahel would create an obligation of blood-revenge (gōʾēl haddām, the blood-avenger) in Joab. Abner is trying to prevent the blood-feud that will eventually consume him in 3:27.
2.23: The killing: wayyakkēhû ʾaḇnēr bᵉʾaḥᵒrê haḥᵃnît ʾel-haḥōmeš — 'Abner struck him with the butt end of the spear into the stomach.' The phrase ʾaḥᵒrê haḥᵃnît (the hind part / butt end of the spear) specifies the non-blade end — Abner uses the shaft rather than the point, possibly attempting a non-lethal stop, but the thrust ruptures the abdomen. The noun ḥōmeš is a precise anatomical term: it appears again in 3:27 (Joab's murder of Abner) and 4:6 (murder of Ishbosheth), forming a grim pattern. In v.23 the death is accidental/defensive; in 3:27 it is premeditated murder — the same anatomical strike, different moral character.
2.24: The pursuit continues: wᵉyôʾāḇ wᵉʾᵃḇîšay rādᵃpû ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr. The place-name giḇʿat ʾammāh (Hill of Ammah) is unidentified geographically; the setting sun establishes the temporal frame of the pursuit ending at dusk — a common narrative boundary marker (cf. 1 Sam 17:52; 2 Sam 3:35).
2.25: The Benjaminites rally around Abner: wayyitqabbᵉṣû ḇnê-ḇinyāmîn ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr wayhyû lᵉʾaggûḏāh ʾeḥāt wayyaʿᵃmᵉḏû ʿal-rōʾš giḇʿāh ʾeḥāt — 'they formed into a single unit and stood their ground on the top of a hill.' The noun ʾaggûḏāh (unit, band, bunch) is used for a military formation and also for a bundle of hyssop (Exod 12:22) — the basic image is of things bound together. Their stand on the hilltop is a defensive tactical position.
2.26: Abner's appeal: hᵃlaṣṣāṇîm tōʾkal ḥereb hᵃlōʾ tēḏaʿ kî-mārāh tihyeh bāʾaḥᵃrônāh — 'Must the sword keep killing forever? Do you not know that this will end in bitterness?' The adverb laṣṣāṇ (forever/endlessly) intensifies the rhetorical question. The noun mārāh in kî-mārāh tihyeh means bitterness or distress — Abner is projecting the cost of continued civil war. His rhetorical question ʿad-mātay lōʾ tōʾmar lāʿām lāšûḇ, 'when will you tell the people to stop pursuing their brothers?' introduces the kinship bond — ʾaḥîḵem, 'their brothers' — as the reason for restraint.
2.27: Joab's oath: ḥay hāʾĕlōhîm kî lûlēʾ dibbartā kî-ʾāz min-habboqer niʿᵃlāh hāʿām — 'As God lives, if you had not spoken, the men would have kept up the pursuit until morning.' The oath particle ḥay hāʾĕlōhîm (as God lives) is the standard assertion of truthfulness by appeal to the living God. Joab's counter-assertion is that Abner's earlier bellicosity at Gibeon (v.14) was itself the provocation — framing the entire day's violence as Abner-initiated.
2.28: The trumpet signaling ceasefire: wayyitqaʿ yôʾāḇ baSšôpār wayyaʿᵃmᵉḏû ḵol-hāʿām — 'Joab blew the trumpet and all the people stopped.' The šôpār (ram's horn) is the standard military signal instrument; in the law codes it also marks Jubilee (Lev 25:9) and covenant assembly (Exod 19:16). Its sounding ends the battle — wᵉlōʾ yōsîpû ʿôḏ lirdōp ʾaḥᵃrê yiśrāʾēl, 'they no longer pursued Israel' — but the narrative closure is explicitly limited: the war continues in 3:1.
2.29: Abner's night march through the Arabah: wᵉʾaḇnēr wᵉʾᵃnāšāyw hālaḵû bāʿᵃrāḇāh kol-hallayla hahûʾ. The Arabah is the Jordan rift valley — a difficult terrain requiring a full night of travel. wayyaʿᵃḇᵉrû ʾet-hayyardēn, 'they crossed the Jordan' — back to safety at Mahanaim east of the river. The Bithron (habbiṯrôn, 'the gorge/ravine') is otherwise unidentified but likely a geographic feature in Gilead.
2.30: The casualty report for David's side: nineteen men plus Asahel missing from Joab's force (niqlam meʿaḇdê dāwiḏ tišʿāh ʿāśār ʾîš wᵉʾᵃśāhēl). The specificity of nineteen — with Asahel named separately — indicates an accurate accounting based on an actual muster. The asymmetry with v.31 (360 Benjaminite dead) establishes David's military advantage even at this early stage.
2.31: David's forces struck down 360 of Benjamin/Abner's men — the number is exact and memorable. The specificity likely reflects an archival source. In contrast, Joab's side loses only 19 + Asahel. The lopsided ratio (roughly 18:1) establishes military superiority for David's forces even at this stage of the civil conflict.
2.32: The burial of Asahel in his father's tomb at Bethlehem (bᵉḇêt leḥem bᵉqeḇer ʾāḇîw) closes the episode. Proper burial in the ancestral tomb is the mark of a good death; Asahel receives this honor despite dying in civil war. Joab and Abishai then marched through the night to Hebron — closing the civil war episode with a return to base, not triumph.

3.1: The summary notice: watthî hammilḥāmāh ʾᵃrûḵāh bên bêt šāʾûl ûḇên bêt dāwiḏ — 'there was a long war between the house of Saul and the house of David.' The noun ʾārûḵ (drawn out, extended) from the root ʾāraḵ (to be long, to lengthen) — same root as the promise of long life in the Decalogue. The theological summary follows: dāwiḏ hōlēḵ wᵉḥāzēq wᵉḇêt šāʾûl hōlᵉḵîm wᵉḏalîm — 'David growing stronger and stronger, while the house of Saul grew weaker and weaker.' The paired participles hōlēḵ wᵉḥāzēq / hōlᵉḵîm wᵉḏalîm use the progressive participle construction (hālaḵ + participle) to indicate ongoing, developing trends — not episodic victories but a continuous trajectory.
3.2: The son-list at Hebron begins: wayyiwwālᵉḏû lᵉḏāwiḏ bānîm bᵉḥeḇrôn — 'sons were born to David at Hebron.' The verb yālad (niphal passive: were born to him) used for royal sons always signals succession politics. Amnon by Ahinoam of Jezreel is firstborn — his seniority and later crime (ch 13) cast a long shadow over the list.
3.3: Chileab (also called Daniel in 1 Chr 3:1) by Abigail the Carmelite; Absalom by Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur. The Aramean alliance through Absalom's mother is a significant international detail — Geshur is an Aramean kingdom northeast of the Sea of Galilee. Absalom's foreign royal blood through his mother (bat-talmay melek gᵉšûr) will later influence his political ambitions.
3.4: Adonijah son of Haggith is fourth; Shephatiah son of Abital is fifth. The list moves through six Hebron sons without pausing on any — the names are given precisely because their birth order will matter in succession contests. Adonijah's later claim to the throne (1 Kgs 1:5) rests on his being fourth-born after the deaths of Amnon, Chileab/Daniel, and Absalom.
3.5: Ithream by Eglah, 'David's wife' (ʾēšet dāwiḏ). The designation is odd — all the other wives are identified by name or origin, not by the redundant 'wife of David.' Some have seen this as a term of endearment or primary-wife status, but the text gives no further indication. The number six mirrors the standard ancient Near Eastern pattern of listing a king's sons from his first six wives in his primary seat of power.
3.6: The narrative pivot: wayhî bihyôt hammilḥāmāh bên bêt šāʾûl ûḇên bêt dāwiḏ wᵉʾaḇnēr hāyāh mitḥazzēq bᵉḇêt šāʾûl — 'while the war continued, Abner was consolidating his power within the house of Saul.' The verb mitḥazzēq (hithpael participle of ḥāzaq) means to strengthen oneself, to make oneself powerful — the reflexive form indicates self-aggrandizement. The same root was used in 3:1 of David's increasing strength; now Abner is described as doing the same thing from within Saul's house, which is the problem Ishbosheth perceives.
3.7: The political dynamite: wᵉlᵉšāʾûl pîlegeš wûšmāh riṣpāh bat-ʾayāh — 'Saul had a concubine named Rizpah daughter of Aiah.' The noun pîlegeš (concubine) designates a secondary wife of lower social status but with full legal standing in the household. In ANE succession practice, possession of the former king's harem signified rightful succession to his throne (cf. 2 Sam 16:21-22 where Absalom takes David's concubines as a statement of kingship). Ishbosheth's charge — māddûaʿ bāʾtā ʾel-pîlegeš ʾāḇî, 'why have you slept with my father's concubine?' — is therefore not a moral complaint but a political accusation of usurpation.
3.8: Abner's furious response: wayyiḥar lᵉʾaḇnēr mᵉʾōḏ ʿal-diḇrê ʾîšbōšet — the phrase wayyiḥar (his anger burned/flared) is the standard idiom for explosive anger (Gen 4:5-6; 30:2; Exod 4:14). Abner's rhetorical question uses the self-deprecating idiom: hᵃrōʾš keleb ʾānōḵî ʾᵃšer lîhûḏāh — 'am I a dog's head, belonging to Judah?' The phrase rōʾš keleb (dog's head) is an expression of worthlessness or contemptibility — a dog was viewed as a scavenging, unclean animal (cf. 1 Sam 17:43; 24:14). The irony is pointed: a man Abner views as trivial has called him a traitor.
3.9: Abner's oath: kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh ʾĕlōhîm lᵉʾaḇnēr wᵉḵōh yōsîp lô kî kaʾᵃšer nišbaʿ YHWH lᵉḏāwiḏ kî-ḵēn ʾeʿᵃśeh-llô — 'God do so to me, and more, if I do not do for David what the LORD swore to him.' The formula kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh ʾĕlōhîm + wᵉḵōh yōsîp is a self-imprecatory oath formula: 'may God do this and add to it' (i.e., unspecified punishment falls on me if I break this). The reference to YHWH's oath to David (nišbaʿ YHWH lᵉḏāwiḏ) — there is no explicit earlier oath in the narrative, but 1 Sam 16:12-13 and various prophetic declarations are likely in view. Abner acknowledges YHWH's sovereign choice before he acts on it.
3.10: The scope of the transfer: lᵉhaʿᵃḇîr hammamlaḵāh mibêt šāʾûl ûlᵉhāqîm ʾet-kiseʾ dāwiḏ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl wᵉʿal-yᵉhûḏāh mîddān wᵉʿaḏ-bᵉʾēr šāḇaʿ — 'to transfer the kingdom from the house of Saul and establish David's throne over Israel and Judah, from Dan to Beersheba.' The formula mîddān wᵉʿaḏ-bᵉʾēr šāḇaʿ (from Dan to Beersheba) is the standard idiom for the full extent of the land — Dan at the northern limit, Beersheba at the southern (Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 3:20; 2 Sam 17:11; 24:2). The double object — 'over Israel AND over Judah' — reiterates the divided political reality that has characterized the narrative since 2:4.
3.11: Ishbosheth's incapacity: wᵉlōʾ yāḵol ʿôḏ lᵉhāšîḇ ʾet-ʾaḇnēr dāḇār miyyirᵉʾātô ʾōtô — 'he could not say another word to Abner, because he was afraid of him.' The verb yārēʾ (to fear) expresses paralysis before a more powerful figure — the king is literally speechless with fear of his own general. The word miyyirᵉʾātô is the noun yirʾāh in construct with a pronominal suffix: 'from his fear of him.' This one clause encapsulates the entire problem: the king has power in name only.
3.12: Abner's opening to David: lᵉmî ʾāreṣ — 'to whom does the land belong?' The rhetorical question is Abner's way of conceding the theological and practical reality: YHWH has determined that the land belongs to David. The verb in kᵉrōt-bᵉrîtᵉḵā ʾittî — 'make a treaty with me' — uses kārat bᵉrît (to cut a covenant) as always. Abner offers his hand (wᵉhinnēh yādî ʿimmaḵ, 'my hand is with you') — a gesture of personal pledge and alliance.
3.13: David's one condition: lōʾ tirʾeh ʾet-pānay kî-ʾim-lᵉpānêḵā tāḇîʾ ʾet-mîḵal bat-šāʾûl — 'you shall not see my face unless you first bring Michal daughter of Saul.' The verb phrase rāʾāh pānîm (to see the face) is the idiom for an audience before a superior. David insists on Michal before receiving Abner — the political logic is clear: Michal's return reconstitutes David's marital connection to the house of Saul, providing a claim of legitimacy through the former king's daughter.
3.14: David sends a parallel demand directly to Ishbosheth: return Michal, for whom 'I paid the bride-price of one hundred foreskins of the Philistines' (ʾašer ʾēraśtî lî bᵉmēʾāh ʿorlôt pᵉlištîm). The noun môhar (bride-price, used in 1 Sam 18:25) is not used here — instead the payment is recalled by description. The act of paying the bride-price establishes ongoing marital rights that override Saul's reassignment of Michal to Palti/Phaltiel (1 Sam 25:44).
3.15: Ishbosheth's compliance — wayyišlaḥ ʾîšbōšet wayyiqāḥehā, 'he sent and took her' — is immediate and unilateral. He takes his sister from her husband Phaltiel/Palti son of Laish by executive fiat, without Phaltiel's consent — an act that emphasizes Ishbosheth's authority over his own house even while helpless before Abner in other matters.
3.16: The most pathos-laden detail in the chapter: wayyēleḵ ʾittāh ʾîšāh hālaḵ ûḇāḵōh ʾaḥᵃrêhā — 'her husband followed her, weeping as he walked behind her.' The participle hālaḵ ûḇāḵōh is the participle + infinitive absolute construction indicating persistent ongoing action: he walked-and-wept, the weeping inseparable from the walking. Abner's curt dismissal — šûḇ lēḵ, 'go back' — ends both Phaltiel's grief and his narrative appearance. The scene is the human cost of the political transaction David and Abner have arranged.
3.17: Abner's diplomatic groundwork: wᵉḏiḇar ʾaḇnēr hāyāh ʿim-ziqnê yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr — 'Abner had been in contact with the elders of Israel, saying.' The pluperfect (hāyāh + qal perfect in narrative context) indicates previous action: this had been happening even before the Michal transaction. The elders' stated desire — gam-timol ʾešmōl ʿet dāwiḏ lᵉmelek ʿᵃlênû, 'you have long wanted David to be king over us' — acknowledges that popular sentiment was already with David.
3.18: Abner cites a YHWH oracle: waYHWH ʾāmar ʾel-dāwiḏ bᵉyad dāwiḏ ʿaḇdî hôšîaʿ ʾet-ʿammî yiśrāʾēl miyyaḏ pᵉlištîm ûmiyyaḏ kol-ʾōyᵉḇêhem — 'by the hand of my servant David I will save my people Israel from the hand of the Philistines and from the hand of all their enemies.' This oracle is not found verbatim in 1 Samuel but reflects the substance of 1 Sam 9:16-17 and 16:12-13. The phrase ʿaḇdî (my servant) is the specific designation of a YHWH-chosen covenant agent — used of Moses, Joshua, and pre-eminently of David (Ps 89:3, 20).
3.19: The special mention of Benjamin — wᵉḏibber ʾaḇnēr gam bᵉʾoznê ḇinyāmîn, 'Abner also spoke in the hearing of Benjamin' — signals the political sensitivity. Benjamin was Saul's own tribe; securing their assent was the most difficult and most necessary part of any transfer of loyalty. The verb dibbēr bᵉʾoznê (spoke in the ears of) indicates private consultation rather than public address.
3.20: The feast at Hebron: wayyāḇōʾ ʾaḇnēr ʾel-dāwiḏ ḥeḇrônāh wᵉʾittô ʿeśrîm ʾᵃnāšîm — 'Abner came to David at Hebron with twenty men.' A delegation of twenty is a significant but not a state-level mission. David's making of a feast (wayyaʿaś dāwiḏ lᵉʾaḇnēr ûlᵃʾnāšîm ʾᵃšer ʾittô mišteh) is the formal hospitality establishing a covenant relationship — mišteh (feast/drinking party) functions as the social seal of agreements in the ANE.
3.21: Abner's final words to David: ʾāqûmāh wᵉʾēlēḵāh wᵉʾeqbeṣāh ʾel-ʾᵃḏōnî hammeleḵ ʾet-kol-yiśrāʾēl wᵉyikrᵉtû ʾitᵉḵā bᵉrît ûmālaḵtā bᵉḵōl ʾᵃšer-tᵃʾawweh napšeḵā — 'let me gather all Israel that they may make a covenant with you and you may reign over all that your heart desires.' The phrase kol ʾᵃšer-tᵃʾawweh napšeḵā (all that your soul/life-force desires) uses nepeš in its meaning of deep desire or appetite — a courtly expression of full submission to the king's will. The departure note — wayyēleḵ bᵉšālôm, 'he went in peace' — is the seal of the agreement, but 'in peace' (šālôm) will be ironic within verses.
3.22: The dramatic arrival of Joab's force: wᵉhinnēh ʾaḇnēr ʾênennû ʿim-dāwiḏ bᵉḥeḇrôn kî šillᵉḥô wayyēleḵ bᵉšālôm — 'Abner was no longer with David at Hebron, for David had sent him away, and he had gone in peace.' The narrator's emphasis — that Abner left 'in peace' and David had 'sent him away' — establishes David's innocence in what follows. The plunder Joab's men bring back (šālāl rāḇ ʾimmām, 'great plunder with them') shows active raiding continuing alongside the diplomatic process.
3.23: The report to Joab: wayyaggîḏû lᵉyôʾāḇ lēʾmōr bāʾ ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr ʾel-hammeleḵ. The phrasing places Joab in the position of being informed after the fact — he was not present at the negotiation, not consulted, not included. His exclusion from the peace treaty with Abner is the narrative's implicit explanation for his subsequent action.
3.24: Joab's direct confrontation: meh ʿāśîtā hinnēh bāʾ ʾaḇnēr ʾēlêḵā lāmmāh-zeh šillᵃḥtô wayyēleḵ hālôḵ — 'what have you done? Abner came to you — why did you send him away? He is gone!' The rhetorical questions carry accusation: the verb hālôḵ after wayyēleḵ is an infinitive absolute used for emphasis — he is gone and gone definitively.
3.25: Joab's intelligence argument: yāḏaʿtā ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr kî lᵉfattôṯᵉḵā bāʾ — 'you know Abner son of Ner — he came to trick you.' The verb pātat (to persuade, entice, seduce) — the same root as Delilah's persuasion of Samson (Judg 14:15; 16:5) — suggests deception through persuasion. Joab's accusation may be genuine intelligence assessment or self-serving rationalization; the narrator presents both without adjudication.
3.26: Joab's action: wayyišlaḥ malʾāḵîm ʾaḥᵃrê ʾaḇnēr wayyāšiḇû ʾōtô mibôr haśśirāh — 'Joab sent messengers after Abner and brought him back from the well of Sirah.' The well of Sirah is otherwise unknown — likely a cistern north of Hebron. The crucial note: wᵉdāwiḏ lōʾ yāḏaʿ, 'David did not know' — the narrator explicitly exonerates David from knowledge of what Joab is planning.
3.27: The murder: wayyaṭṭēhû yôʾāḇ ʾel-tôḵ haššaʿar lᵉdabbēr ʾittô baSšŝeḵel — 'Joab took him aside in the gateway, as if to speak privately with him.' The gateway (šaʿar) is the customary public meeting place; taking someone aside there (ʾel-tôḵ haššaʿar) implies a private conference — but it also marks the space of civic/judicial activity being perverted for murder. wayyakkēhû šām haḥōmeš wayyāmot — 'he struck him there in the stomach and he died.' The noun ḥōmeš appears again (cf. 2:23): the identical blow that killed Asahel is now replicated as premeditated murder. The reason is stated — ʿal-dam ʿᵃśāhēl ʾāḥîw, 'for the blood of Asahel his brother' — but blood vengeance law required the killing of a murderer, and Abner's killing of Asahel in battle was legally defensive (2:19-23), not murder.
3.28: David's formal disavowal: wᵉʾānōḵî ûmamlᵃḵtî nᵉqiyyîm mēYHWH ʿad-ʿôlām middǝmê ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr — 'I and my kingdom are forever innocent before the LORD of the blood of Abner son of Ner.' The legal language is precise: nāqî (innocent, clean-handed) is the term used in innocence declarations in judicial contexts (cf. Gen 44:10; Num 5:28). The prepositional phrase mēYHWH (before/from YHWH) establishes the divine tribunal as the reference point — David is declaring his innocence to YHWH, not merely to political observers. The word ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) marks this as a permanent exculpation, not a situational disclaimer.
3.29: The curse on Joab's house: yāḡūlû ʿal-rōʾš yôʾāḇ wᵉʾel kol-bêt ʾāḇîw — 'let the guilt rest on Joab's head and on all his father's house.' The verb gûl (to be guilty, to bear punishment) in the imperfect jussive calls down curse. The specific afflictions listed — one who has a zāḇ (discharge, bodily flux — possibly gonorrhea or chronic skin disease), one who is leprous, one who falls by the sword, one who lacks bread — are the standard ANE curse catalogue: physical illness, violent death, and famine are the three great threats to life and family continuity. The curse is specific to Joab precisely because David cannot act against him politically at this point (v.39).
3.30: The narrator's explanatory aside: wᵉyôʾāḇ wᵃʾᵃḇîšay ʾāḥîw hārᵃgû ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ʿal ʾᵃšer hēmît ʾet-ʾᵃśāhēl ʾᵃḥîhem bᵉgiḇʿôn bammilḥāmāh — 'Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner because he had killed their brother Asahel at Gibeon in the battle.' The narrator is not justifying the murder but explaining its motivation — the blood-vengeance logic that David's curse (v.29) implicitly rejects as an illegitimate extension of gōʾēl rights beyond their legal scope (killing a man in battle is not murder warranting blood-vengeance).
3.31: David commands public mourning: qirʿû ḇigᵉḏêḵem wᵉḥigru śaqqîm ûsᵉḏû lipnê ʾaḇnēr — 'tear your clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourn before Abner.' The three-part mourning sequence (torn clothing + sackcloth + lamentation) is the standard public grief protocol. Notably, hammelek dāwiḏ hōlēḵ ʾaḥᵃrê hammîṭṭāh — 'King David himself walked behind the bier' — the king as a mourner following the corpse is a striking social inversion of status: the most powerful man walking behind a man he could not protect.
3.32: The burial at Hebron — Abner's remains interred in the city of David's own reign. The public weeping — wayyibk hammeleḵ ʾel-qeḇer ʾaḇnēr wayyibkû kol-hāʿām, 'the king wept at the grave of Abner, and all the people wept' — is communal mourning that functions politically as well as personally. David's public grief serves to demonstrate that he did not want or engineer Abner's death.
3.33: The king's lament over Abner: hᵃkᵉmôt nāḇāl yāmût ʾaḇnēr — 'should Abner have died as a fool dies?' The comparison with nāḇāl (the fool) invokes not just the common noun but the specific person Nabal of 1 Sam 25 — whose name means 'fool' and whose death was sudden, unlamented, and without honor. David is saying: Abner deserved better than a fool's death (death by treachery, without warning, in a place of supposed peace). The implicit contrast: a hero deserves to die in battle, not by a hired killing in a city gateway.
3.34: The elegy continues: yādêḵā lōʾ-ʾᵃsûrôt wᵉraglêḵā lōʾ-lᵉneḥuštayim huggāšt — 'your hands were not bound; your feet were not put in chains.' The passive verbs (ʾāsûr = bound; hugaš = brought/placed) establish the pathos: Abner was not a prisoner who could not defend himself. He walked freely into the killing zone, deceived by the appearance of peace. kinnᵉpōl lipnê ḇᵉnê-ʿawlāh nāpāltā — 'you fell as one falls before treacherous men' — uses the noun ʿawlāh (wickedness, injustice) — the death was not battle-death but judicial murder by treacherous men (ḇᵉnê-ʿawlāh, 'sons of iniquity').
3.35: The people urge David to eat before the day is over: wayyāḇōʾ kol-hāʿām lᵉhaḇrôt ʾet-dāwiḏ leḥem bᵉʿôḏ hayyôm. David's oath — kōh yᵃʿᵃśeh-li ʾĕlōhîm wᵉḵōh yōsîp kî ʾim-lipnê ḇô-hašŝemeš ʾeṭʿam-leḥem ʾô kol-mᵃʾûmāh — uses the same self-imprecatory formula as Abner's oath in v.9. The fasting-until-sunset mirrors David's mourning fast after Saul's death (1:12) — the formal structure of grief enacted through abstinence from food.
3.36: The political effect of David's public mourning: wayyakkîrû kol-hāʿām wayyîṭaḇ bᵉʿênêhem — 'all the people took notice, and it pleased them.' The verb nāḵar (hifil, to recognize/acknowledge) indicates a conscious assessment: they recognized (evaluated) what David was doing and approved. David's public grief succeeds as both genuine mourning and political communication.
3.37: The narrator's summary of the political effect: wayyēḏᵉʿû kol-hāʿām wᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl bayyôm hahûʾ kî lōʾ hāyᵉtāh min-hammeleḵ lᵉhāmît ʾet-ʾaḇnēr ben-nēr — 'so all the people and all Israel understood that day that the king had no part in killing Abner.' The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) in the qal perfect with wayyᵉḏᵉʿû signals cognitive resolution — the people have arrived at a definite conclusion. The legal vocabulary of the disavowal is carried through: lōʾ hāyᵉtāh min-hammeleḵ, 'was not from the king.'
3.38: David's tribute to Abner: hᵃlōʾ tēḏᵉʿû kî-śar wᵉḡāḏôl nāpal hayyôm hazzeh bᵉyiśrāʾēl — 'do you not know that a commander and a great man has fallen in Israel today?' The word pair śar wᵉḡāḏôl (commander and great man) is a standard Hebrew idiom for high-ranking officials (Jer 5:5; 26:10; Dan 9:6). The rhetorical question hᵃlōʾ tēḏᵉʿû calls for acknowledgment of what is objectively true — David is not asking permission to mourn but demanding recognition of what the nation has lost.
3.39: David's admission of weakness: wᵉʾānōḵî hayyôm raḵ wᵉmāšûaḥ melek wᵉhāʾᵃnāšîm hāʾēlleh bᵉnê ṣᵉrûyāh qāšîm mimmennî — 'I am today weak, though anointed king; and these men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too strong for me.' The adjective raḵ (soft, tender, weak) is juxtaposed with māšûaḥ melek (anointed king) — David has the title and the divine anointing, but not yet the political power to control his own military commanders. The verb-less clause qāšîm mimmennî (too hard/strong for me) is a concise admission of political impotence. David concludes with the same appeal to divine justice as the lament's ending: yᵉšallēm YHWH lāʿōśeh hārāʿāh — 'may the LORD repay the evildoer according to his evil.' The verb šālēm (piel: to repay, to requite, to make complete) is the root of šālôm — but here in its retributive meaning: YHWH completes justice by requiting the wrong.
